Peak MPV in 25–36h may better capture ongoing inflammatory/consumptive extremes closer to Hour 60, which can correlate with AKI trajectory. Still indirect, but within this batch it is one of the more plausible keepers alongside 25–36h MPV mean.

Minimum mean platelet volume in the immediate post-op window; MPV is a nonspecific inflammation/platelet activation marker. Weak and indirect for AKI prediction and likely dominated by hemodynamics/lactate/renal labs in a sparse model.
Minimum MPV during 13–24h post-op, reflecting nonspecific platelet activation. Still an indirect signal with low unique value for creatinine trajectory compared with perfusion markers.
Minimum MPV closer to the outcome window, potentially reflecting ongoing systemic stress. Slightly more temporally relevant but remains nonspecific and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Count/frequency of MPV measurements in 0–12h, mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. Not a physiologic renal signal and low priority for sparse modeling.
MPV measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects care intensity rather than kidney function. Adds little unique predictive value and is likely redundant with overall acuity markers.
MPV measurement frequency in 25–36h is a proxy for concern/complexity rather than a biomarker. Generally low-yield for sparse selection versus physiologic extremes like lactate.

Average lactate in the first 12h reflects global hypoperfusion/low cardiac output after surgery, a risk factor for AKI. Useful but early and smoothed, and often dominated by lactate extremes and later windows.

Mean lactate in 13–24h captures sustained perfusion deficit beyond immediate bypass/anesthesia effects. Stronger temporal relevance and can add unique signal about ongoing shock burden linked to AKI risk.
Peak lactate in 0–12h captures acute hypoperfusion/ischemia-reperfusion severity, which can precipitate AKI. High-value extreme, though somewhat noisy early post-op compared with later trajectory.

Peak lactate in 13–24h reflects persistent or recurrent hypoperfusion closer to the outcome window. This extreme is a top sparse-model candidate as a strong, near-direct precursor of renal hypoperfusion and evolving AKI.
Minimum lactate in 0–12h mainly reflects best observed perfusion state and is less informative than peaks for injury risk. Likely redundant once max/mean lactate are included.
Minimum lactate in 13–24h indicates periods of adequate perfusion but does not capture insult severity. Generally dominated by lactate max/mean for predicting creatinine rise.
Lactate measurement frequency in 0–12h is a proxy for instability/monitoring intensity. May weakly correlate with severity but is indirect and lower priority than actual lactate values.
Lactate measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects clinician concern for shock/poor perfusion. Still a proxy and typically redundant with lactate level features in sparse selection.
Lactate measurement frequency in 25–36h may indicate ongoing instability closer to Hour 60. Modestly informative as a severity proxy but inferior to physiologic lactate extremes/means.

Mean alkaline phosphatase in 0–12h reflects cholestasis/liver/bone signal and is not a direct renal perfusion or filtration marker. Low expected unique value for creatinine ratio and likely noise in this context.

Peak alkaline phosphatase early post-op reflects hepatobiliary/cholestatic signal more than renal function. In a sparse AKI model it is indirect and likely noise/redundant with broader illness markers.
Minimum alkaline phosphatase in the first 12h has little physiologic linkage to evolving AKI. Low priority and dominated by more kidney-proximal hemodynamic/lab features not in this family.
Count of ALP measurements is mainly a monitoring/clinician-behavior proxy rather than a biomarker. Early (0–12h) measurement frequency is especially nonspecific for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
ALP measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects care intensity or concern for liver issues, not kidney injury. Unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio in a sparse model.

Later ALP measurement frequency (25–36h) could weakly proxy ongoing complications prompting labs, but remains indirect. Slightly higher only due to closer timing to Hour 60, still low sparse-selection priority.

Mean RDW is a nonspecific marker of baseline frailty/inflammation and transfusion/bleeding context rather than direct renal physiology. Early window and redundancy with later RDW summaries make it a weak sparse candidate.
RDW mean at 13–24h remains a broad severity/frailty signal with limited mechanistic specificity for AKI. Some prognostic value, but likely dominated by the closest (25–36h) RDW if any RDW is kept.

Later RDW mean may capture sustained anemia/transfusion/inflammation burden closer to the creatinine outcome. Still indirect for kidney injury, but the best-timed RDW summary and could survive screening if few labs are available.
Peak RDW in 0–12h is nonspecific and may reflect perioperative transfusion/hemoconcentration artifacts. Extremes can matter, but early timing and indirectness limit sparse-model value.
RDW max at 13–24h is a general illness/transfusion proxy rather than a renal insult marker. Some signal possible, but largely redundant with 25–36h RDW extremes.

RDW max at 25–36h captures the most extreme anisocytosis/anemia signal closest to Hour 60 and may proxy ongoing bleeding/transfusion/inflammation that correlates with AKI risk. Among RDW variants, this extreme is a reasonable sparse candidate.
Minimum RDW is rarely clinically meaningful and does not represent an acute renal-relevant insult. Early timing and low interpretability make it a poor sparse feature.
RDW minimum at 13–24h adds little beyond baseline hematologic status and is not a plausible driver of creatinine rise. Redundant with mean/max RDW and low priority.
Later RDW minimum still lacks a clear pathophysiologic link to AKI and is dominated by RDW max/mean if RDW is used at all. Unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Measurement count is a monitoring-intensity proxy (CBC frequency) rather than a physiologic predictor. In 0–12h it mostly reflects routine post-op labs and adds minimal unique value.
CBC/RDW measurement frequency at 13–24h reflects clinician behavior and complexity of care, not kidney function. Low specificity and unlikely to be retained in a sparse linear model.

Counts how often RDW was checked in 25–36h, reflecting monitoring intensity rather than renal physiology. As a clinician-behavior proxy with weak specificity for AKI, it is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Average chloride in the immediate 0–12h post-op period, influenced by bypass/fluids and early shifts. Likely redundant with later chloride summaries and less temporally relevant to Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Mean chloride at 13–24h reflects ongoing fluid/acid-base management but is a nonspecific AKI signal. In a sparse model it is typically dominated by later (25–36h) chloride and/or extreme values.

Mean chloride at 25–36h captures sustained hyperchloremia from resuscitation that can correlate with renal stress and illness trajectory. It has better temporal proximity than earlier means, though still indirect and often weaker than extremes.
Peak chloride in 0–12h may reflect large chloride load early after surgery. Early peaks are noisy and less predictive than later peaks, so it is a lower-priority redundant version.
Peak chloride at 13–24h better reflects ongoing chloride exposure and evolving acid-base status than 0–12h. Still an indirect renal-risk marker, but the extreme can add more signal than the mean.

Peak chloride at 25–36h is the closest extreme measure of hyperchloremia, a plausible marker of heavy saline exposure/acid-base disturbance linked to AKI risk. Among chloride features, this extreme and timing make it the strongest sparse-model candidate.
Lowest chloride in 0–12h is often driven by dilutional effects and peri-bypass shifts rather than kidney injury. It is a weak, noisy signal and dominated by later chloride information.
Minimum chloride at 13–24h is generally less pathophysiologically tied to AKI than hyperchloremia and often reflects dilution/measurement variability. Low unique value for sparse selection.
Minimum chloride at 25–36h is temporally closer but still a less specific renal-risk signal than peak chloride. May add minor information about dilution/volume status but is usually redundant.
Number of chloride measurements in 0–12h mainly reflects ICU monitoring intensity and workflow. As a proxy variable with limited physiologic specificity, it is low priority.
Measurement frequency of chloride at 13–24h is primarily clinician behavior/acuity proxy rather than a renal biomarker. Unlikely to add unique predictive value beyond actual chloride values.

More frequent chloride checks at 25–36h may indicate ongoing instability or electrolyte/acid-base management, modestly correlating with AKI risk. Still largely a monitoring proxy and typically inferior to physiologic chloride extremes.

Whether HDL was measured in 0–12h reflects ordering patterns (often routine lipid history) rather than acute renal physiology. Very unlikely to help predict Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse model.
HDL measurement at 13–24h is a clinician-ordering proxy with minimal mechanistic link to evolving postoperative AKI. Low uniqueness and low expected predictive value.
HDL measurement at 25–36h remains a behavior/routine-care indicator rather than a kidney injury signal. Not a plausible sparse-model keeper for creatinine ratio prediction.

Average neutrophil percentage reflects early postoperative inflammatory/stress response. It is an indirect AKI signal and likely redundant with other severity markers; early window also noisy for Hour-60 creatinine.

Mean neutrophil percentage in 13–24h captures ongoing inflammation/infection risk. Still indirect for renal dysfunction and unlikely to survive sparse selection versus hemodynamics/renal-specific markers.
Peak neutrophil percentage may reflect acute stress response or early infection signal. However it is nonspecific for AKI and early post-op peaks are common, limiting unique predictive value.

A higher peak neutrophil percentage in 13–24h may better indicate evolving infection/systemic inflammation than the mean. Among neutrophil summaries this extreme is the most plausible keeper, but remains indirect for creatinine ratio.
Minimum neutrophil percentage is rarely a meaningful renal hypoperfusion/AKI precursor in this context. Likely dominated by max/mean and by other illness-severity variables.
Minimum neutrophil percentage in 13–24h is not a strong mechanistic driver of AKI and is hard to interpret clinically. Redundant and unlikely to add unique signal in a sparse model.
Count of measurements is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. It does not directly encode physiology and is weak for sparse selection.
Measurement frequency reflects care intensity or concern rather than kidney function. Typically redundant with overall acuity and should be deprioritized.
Later measurement frequency (25–36h) may weakly proxy evolving complications prompting repeat labs. Still a behavior/monitoring signal rather than a biomarker, so only modest priority.

Mean albumin reflects hemodilution, capillary leak, and baseline nutritional/inflammatory status, which correlate with AKI risk. Useful but early and somewhat nonspecific; likely redundant with fluid balance/illness severity.
Maximum albumin in 0–12h is less informative than low values because it can reflect timing of draws and dilution effects. Likely dominated by albumin_min/mean for AKI risk.

Minimum albumin in 0–12h captures worst hemodilution/capillary leak and low oncotic pressure states associated with renal hypoperfusion and AKI. Within the albumin family, the extreme low is the most informative for sparse selection.
Albumin measurement count is primarily a proxy for clinician practice/monitoring intensity. It adds little direct physiologic information for predicting creatinine ratio.
Frequency of albumin checks reflects care patterns (e.g., nutrition/volume assessment) more than renal physiology. Low priority for a sparse linear model.
Later albumin measurement frequency may indicate ongoing instability or fluid/oncotic management closer to the outcome window. Still largely a behavior proxy, so only modest value.
Whether triglycerides were measured is a clinician-driven indicator (e.g., propofol monitoring) rather than a renal biomarker. Very weak and unlikely to contribute unique predictive signal.

Whether triglycerides were checked in 13–24h is mainly a monitoring/clinician-behavior proxy (nutrition/propofol/lipid concern), not a direct renal physiology signal. Unlikely to add unique value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Triglyceride measurement in 25–36h reflects care intensity or propofol/nutrition management rather than kidney injury. Even though closer to the target window, it is still a weak, indirect proxy and low priority for sparse selection.

Average hemoglobin in the immediate post-op window reflects hemodilution/bleeding/transfusion and oxygen delivery, which can relate indirectly to renal hypoperfusion. Early (0–12h) values are noisy and this mean is dominated by later minima for AKI risk, so low sparse priority.

Mean hemoglobin at 13–24h captures ongoing anemia/hemodilution but smooths over clinically important nadirs. Likely redundant with hemoglobin_min (especially later) and only indirectly linked to AKI, so weak keeper.

Mean hemoglobin at 25–36h is closer to Hour 60 and may reflect sustained anemia affecting renal oxygen delivery. Still less informative than the nadir (min) for capturing ischemic insult, so moderate but not top-tier.

Peak hemoglobin in 0–12h is usually driven by transfusion/hemoconcentration and is not a typical driver of AKI risk compared with anemia/hypotension. Early timing and limited mechanistic link make it a poor sparse-model candidate.

Maximum hemoglobin in 13–24h mostly reflects transfusion/volume status rather than renal injury trajectory. Adds little unique predictive signal versus minima/clinical instability measures, so low priority.
Maximum hemoglobin in 25–36h is not a strong precursor of AKI and is often a treatment artifact (transfusion/diuresis). Even near-term, it is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Lowest hemoglobin in 0–12h captures acute anemia/bleeding/hemodilution that can contribute to renal hypoxia. However, immediate post-op nadirs can be transient/noisy and are less predictive than later minima, so moderate.

Hemoglobin nadir in 13–24h better reflects persistent anemia after initial fluid shifts and is more plausibly linked to renal oxygen delivery and evolving AKI. Among hemoglobin summaries, the minimum is the most informative aggregation and a strong sparse-model candidate.

Hemoglobin nadir in 25–36h is the closest window and best captures ongoing anemia at a time when AKI is evolving, making it a strong near-term predictor of Hour-60 creatinine rise. It is the dominant hemoglobin feature here (extreme + temporal proximity) and high priority for sparse selection.

Number/frequency of hemoglobin checks in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring intensity/bleeding concern proxy. Early post-op measurement frequency is routine and noisy, offering little unique predictive value.

Hemoglobin measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects clinician concern (bleeding/transfusion) more than renal physiology. Likely redundant with actual hemoglobin values and other severity markers, so low priority.

More frequent hemoglobin checks in 25–36h may indicate ongoing instability/bleeding and could weakly correlate with complications that affect kidneys. Still a proxy variable and generally dominated by the hemoglobin nadir itself, so only slight value.

Whether TEG fibrinogen activity was measured early reflects bleeding/coagulopathy evaluation and clinician behavior rather than direct renal injury. In a sparse model for creatinine ratio, this is a weak, indirect proxy.

TEG fibrinogen activity measurement in 13–24h indicates ongoing hemostasis assessment (bleeding/transfusion management) and is not a direct kidney function marker. Unlikely to add unique predictive signal beyond anemia/transfusion/hemodynamics features.

Whether TEG fibrinogen activity was checked in 25–36h is mainly a monitoring/bleeding-management proxy. It is indirect for AKI risk and unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio in a sparse model.

Mean ACT in the immediate post-op window reflects anticoagulation/heparin reversal and procedural context more than renal physiology. Likely redundant with ACT extremes and weakly linked to Hour-60 creatinine trajectory.

Peak ACT may capture excessive anticoagulation or ongoing coagulopathy early after surgery, which can correlate with complexity/bleeding and downstream AKI risk. Still indirect and early (0–12h), so a low-priority sparse feature.

Minimum ACT early post-op is usually driven by protocolized reversal/measurement timing rather than kidney injury biology. Adds little unique signal beyond other ACT summaries and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Frequency of ACT measurement is a clinician-behavior/monitoring-intensity proxy (e.g., bleeding concern, procedural complexity). Not a direct physiologic marker for AKI and typically dominated by hemodynamics/renal labs.
ACT measurement count in 13–24h mainly reflects ongoing monitoring for anticoagulation/bleeding rather than renal perfusion or injury. Low unique value for predicting Hour-60 creatinine_ratio.

Late (25–36h) continued ACT monitoring can indicate ongoing complications (bleeding, anticoagulation needs) that may co-occur with AKI risk. Still a proxy variable, but timing closer to Hour 60 gives slightly more potential signal than earlier epochs.

Mean AST in 0–12h reflects early hepatic/ischemic injury and overall operative insult, which can correlate with multi-organ dysfunction including AKI. However it is early and averaged (smoothed), so it is a weaker sparse-model candidate than an extreme.

Peak AST in 0–12h can capture ischemic hepatitis/low-flow states and severity of perioperative shock, a plausible precursor to AKI. Among AST summaries, the max best preserves the insult signal, though it remains indirect and early.
Minimum AST is not clinically meaningful for organ injury and is largely noise/assay baseline. It is dominated by AST max/mean and offers negligible unique predictive value.

AST measurement frequency is primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy (concern for liver injury/hemolysis) rather than a biomarker itself. Typically redundant with actual AST values and weak for sparse selection.
Counting AST checks in 13–24h reflects clinician concern and care complexity more than physiology. Unlikely to add independent predictive value beyond hemodynamics and lab values.

Later AST monitoring (25–36h) may indicate evolving organ dysfunction and could weakly track severity relevant to AKI. Still a proxy variable and generally dominated by actual AST levels and renal/hemodynamic features.

Mean total protein early post-op reflects hemodilution, capillary leak, and nutritional/inflammatory status, which can relate to fluid balance and AKI risk. It is nonspecific and early, so only a modest sparse-model candidate.
Maximum total protein in 0–12h is less informative for renal injury and can be driven by sampling/hemoconcentration artifacts. Likely redundant with mean/min and low priority for sparse selection.

Minimum total protein in 0–12h can better capture hemodilution/capillary leak and low oncotic pressure, which may relate to aggressive fluid resuscitation and renal congestion risk. Within this family, the min is the most potentially informative extreme, though still indirect and early.

Count/frequency of total protein labs in 0–12h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity rather than renal physiology. Weak and early, and dominated by more direct kidney/hemodynamic signals in sparse selection.
Measurement frequency of total protein in 13–24h is a clinician-behavior/acuity proxy, not a direct AKI precursor. Unlikely to add unique value in a sparse linear model.
Later (25–36h) monitoring frequency may weakly track evolving complications prompting labs, but still indirect. Could add small incremental signal as an intensity-of-care proxy, but low priority.

Whether HbA1c was checked early post-op reflects preop diabetes assessment/documentation rather than acute renal trajectory. Mostly redundant with known diabetes/comorbidity features and not time-varying.
HbA1c measurement flag in 13–24h is a workflow/monitoring artifact and not mechanistically linked to Hour-60 creatinine changes. Very unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.
Late HbA1c measurement is still a non-physiologic proxy and does not capture evolving AKI. Adds little unique predictive value versus baseline comorbidities.
Mean serum bicarbonate/total CO2 in 0–12h reflects acid–base status but is noisy immediately post-bypass and resuscitation. Likely redundant with later CO2 and lactate/hemodynamics for AKI risk.
Mean CO2 in 13–24h captures more stable metabolic acidosis/alkalosis that can accompany hypoperfusion or renal dysfunction. Moderately useful but still less sensitive than extremes and later window.

Mean CO2 in 25–36h is closer to the Hour-60 target and reflects sustained metabolic derangement that often tracks evolving AKI. Stronger sparse-model candidate among CO2 summaries due to timing and stability.
Peak CO2 (metabolic alkalosis) early post-op is often driven by ventilation/diuretics/transfusion and is less tied to AKI evolution. Low unique value and early-window noise.
Higher CO2 in 13–24h may reflect contraction alkalosis/diuretic effect and is an indirect signal for renal outcomes. Generally weaker than low CO2 (acidosis) and later measures for AKI prediction.

Late peak CO2 can indicate alkalosis from diuresis or ventilation changes, which is not a primary AKI driver. Some contextual value but typically dominated by CO2 minima/means and direct renal markers.
Minimum CO2 early captures episodes of metabolic acidosis that can reflect hypoperfusion, but 0–12h is confounded by immediate post-op physiology. Useful extreme, yet timing limits sparse priority.

Minimum CO2 in 13–24h captures clinically meaningful metabolic acidosis episodes linked to shock/poor perfusion and impending AKI. Stronger than mean/max for preserving insult signal and reasonably timed.

Minimum CO2 in 25–36h is the closest extreme acid–base marker to Hour 60 and can reflect ongoing hypoperfusion or reduced renal acid handling. High unique predictive value among this batch and most likely to survive sparse selection.

Frequency of CO2 measurements in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring/acuity proxy rather than a physiologic biomarker. Could weakly correlate with instability but is generally low priority versus actual CO2 values.

Count/frequency of CO2 measurements in 13–24h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/acuity proxy rather than a renal physiology signal. Likely redundant with other severity markers and weak for sparse selection.

Frequency of CO2 checks in 25–36h may reflect ongoing ventilatory/acid–base instability closer to the outcome window. Still largely clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy, so only a modest sparse-model candidate.
Whether BNP was measured in 0–12h reflects clinician concern for heart failure/volume status rather than a direct kidney injury marker. Early post-op timing and proxy nature make it low priority.

BNP measurement in 13–24h suggests concern for congestion/low output that can contribute to AKI. However it is still a testing-behavior proxy (not the BNP value) and likely redundant with hemodynamics/diuretics/fluids.

BNP measurement in 25–36h is a later proxy for suspected cardiac dysfunction/volume overload, which can relate to renal outcomes. Still indirect and dominated by actual physiologic/renal markers, so not a top sparse feature.

Mean PaCO2 in 0–12h reflects ventilation/anesthesia effects and early post-bypass physiology more than evolving AKI. Mean aggregation and early timing make it weak and redundant with later PaCO2 summaries.

Mean PaCO2 in 13–24h captures sustained ventilatory/acid–base status that can correlate with illness severity. Still indirect for kidney injury and likely less informative than extremes or later-window values.

Mean PaCO2 in 25–36h is closer to Hour-60 and may reflect ongoing respiratory failure/acid–base burden associated with worse renal trajectory. As a smoothed measure it is still indirect and often redundant with PaCO2 extremes.

Peak PaCO2 in 0–12h may indicate early hypoventilation/respiratory failure and associated acidosis, which can track with severity. Early post-op noise limits specificity for later creatinine ratio, so only modest value.

Peak PaCO2 in 13–24h better captures clinically meaningful hypercapnic episodes (and likely acidosis) after immediate post-op stabilization. Extremes can add unique severity/physiology signal beyond means, making it a moderate sparse candidate.

Peak PaCO2 in 25–36h is the closest extreme measure and may reflect ongoing respiratory failure/acidemia linked to hemodynamic stress and AKI risk. Still an indirect renal predictor, but among PaCO2 features this is one of the more informative.
Minimum PaCO2 in 0–12h often reflects transient hyperventilation/ventilator settings immediately post-op. It is early, indirect, and likely noisy/redundant, so low sparse-selection priority.

Minimum PaCO2 in 13–24h may reflect episodes of hyperventilation or compensation for metabolic acidosis. It is still indirect for kidney injury and generally less informative than hypercapnic extremes for severity.

Minimum PaCO2 in 25–36h is temporally closer and may indicate compensatory hyperventilation in response to metabolic acidosis. However it remains an indirect signal and is typically weaker than max PaCO2 for capturing respiratory failure burden.
Frequency of PaCO2 measurements in 0–12h mainly reflects routine post-op ABG monitoring and clinician behavior. High redundancy and weak physiologic specificity for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

PaCO2 measurement frequency in 13–24h is largely a proxy for monitoring intensity/acuity rather than a direct renal risk factor. Unlikely to survive sparse selection when actual physiologic values are available.
Count/frequency of pCO2 measurements in 25–36h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/ventilation-management proxy. It is indirect for AKI risk and unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model.
Whether indirect bilirubin was checked in 0–12h reflects clinician behavior/concern rather than a renal biomarker. Early post-op timing is noisy and this is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Indicator that indirect bilirubin was measured in 13–24h is still primarily a monitoring proxy. Any association with AKI would be indirect (illness severity/hemolysis) and dominated by direct renal/hemodynamic features.

Indirect bilirubin measurement in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing hemolysis/hepatic dysfunction and overall complexity closer to the outcome window. Still not a physiologic value and likely redundant with stronger severity markers, so low priority.

Mean calcium in 0–12h reflects early electrolyte status influenced by transfusion, citrate, and bypass-related shifts. It is indirect for kidney injury and early means are noisy and redundant with later/extreme calcium summaries.

Mean calcium in 13–24h captures more stable electrolyte management than the immediate post-op period. However, mean values smooth extremes and calcium is an indirect AKI signal, so it is a weak sparse-model candidate.

Mean calcium in 25–36h is the closest window and may reflect sustained derangements from renal dysfunction, citrate load, or critical illness. Still indirect and likely less informative than extremes, but could add modest unique signal.

Peak calcium in 0–12h is often driven by peri-bypass supplementation and transient shifts. It is unlikely to be a stable predictor of Hour-60 creatinine ratio and is dominated by later calcium features.

Peak calcium in 13–24h may reflect aggressive replacement or evolving metabolic issues. It remains an indirect AKI marker and is less compelling than minima or later-window summaries for sparse selection.

Peak calcium in 25–36h is temporally closer but hypercalcemia is not a typical direct precursor of AKI in this setting. Likely reflects treatment patterns and is less informative than calcium minima for renal hypoperfusion/critical illness.

Minimum calcium in 0–12h can capture early hypocalcemia from citrate/transfusion and severity, but this period is confounded by immediate post-op physiology. It is indirect for AKI and likely redundant with later minima.

Minimum calcium in 13–24h may better reflect persistent hypocalcemia associated with transfusion burden, inflammation, and organ dysfunction. As an extreme it preserves insult signal, but remains an indirect predictor.

Minimum calcium in 25–36h is the most temporally proximate extreme and can reflect ongoing critical illness, transfusion/citrate effects, and evolving organ dysfunction that tracks with AKI risk. Among calcium features, this is the strongest sparse-model candidate due to timing and use of an extreme.

Number of calcium measurements in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring/treatment-intensity proxy in a highly protocolized period. It is not a direct physiologic signal and is low priority for sparse selection.

Measurement frequency of calcium in 13–24h reflects clinician behavior and complexity rather than calcium derangement itself. It is likely redundant with actual calcium values and other severity indicators.

More frequent calcium checks in 25–36h may indicate ongoing instability or replacement needs closer to the outcome window. Still a proxy variable and generally dominated by the calcium extreme/value features, so only modest priority.

Mean PTT in the immediate post-op window reflects anticoagulation/heparin effect and bleeding risk more than renal physiology. Early and averaged signal is likely noisy and largely redundant with later/extreme PTT summaries for sparse selection.

Mean PTT at 25–36h captures sustained anticoagulation/coagulopathy later in the ICU course, which can correlate with severity/organ dysfunction. Still indirect for AKI and likely dominated by extreme PTT values, so moderate priority.

Peak PTT early post-op often reflects residual heparin, transfusion, or bypass-related coagulopathy. It is an indirect severity/management marker and early timing limits unique value for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.

Peak PTT at 25–36h may indicate ongoing anticoagulation or evolving coagulopathy/critical illness closer to the outcome window. As an extreme it is more informative than the mean, but remains an indirect proxy for renal injury.

Minimum PTT early post-op is rarely a meaningful renal hypoperfusion/AKI precursor and may reflect lab/assay variation or brief normalization. It is dominated by later and/or maximal PTT signals for sparse modeling.
Minimum PTT at 25–36h is not a strong mechanistic predictor of AKI and is less informative than maxima/means for coagulopathy burden. Likely redundant/noisy and low priority for a sparse feature set.

Count of PTT measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy (e.g., concern for bleeding/heparin titration). Early measurement frequency is weakly tied to future creatinine and should be deprioritized.

PTT measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects care complexity and anticoagulation management rather than kidney function. As a proxy variable it is unlikely to survive sparse selection when physiologic features are available.
More frequent PTT checks at 25–36h can indicate ongoing instability/anticoagulation needs closer to the 60h outcome. Still a proxy rather than a biomarker, so only modest value.

Mean monocyte count early post-op reflects nonspecific inflammatory response and perioperative stress. It is indirect for AKI and likely redundant with stronger inflammation/illness markers not in this batch.

Mean monocyte count at 13–24h may capture evolving inflammation/infection risk that can contribute to AKI. However it is nonspecific and typically weak compared with hemodynamics/renal markers, so low-moderate priority.

Peak monocyte count in 0–12h is largely driven by immediate postoperative inflammatory shifts and lab variability. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value for 60h creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Peak monocyte count at 13–24h could reflect heightened inflammatory activation, which may correlate with later organ dysfunction. Still indirect and generally weaker than direct renal perfusion/function signals, so modest score.
Minimum monocyte count early post-op is not a clear AKI precursor and may reflect dilutional effects or transient leukocyte margination. Low signal-to-noise and redundant within the monocyte family.
Minimum monocyte count at 13–24h is difficult to interpret mechanistically for AKI prediction and is typically less informative than maxima/means. Low priority for sparse selection.

Measurement count is a monitoring-frequency proxy (CBC ordering intensity) rather than a physiologic marker. Early post-op ordering patterns are unlikely to provide unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Count of how often monocytes were checked (monitoring intensity) rather than a physiologic value. Mostly a clinician-behavior proxy with weak, non-specific linkage to AKI and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Frequency of monocyte measurements in the closest window, reflecting care intensity/suspicion more than renal physiology. Still a weak, indirect proxy and dominated by true renal perfusion/function markers.

Average serum phosphorus early post-op; phosphorus can rise with reduced GFR/catabolism but is noisy immediately after bypass and shifts. Mean also smooths peaks, so it is a weaker, more redundant AKI signal.
Average phosphorus in the 13–24h window, which better reflects early renal trajectory than 0–12h. Still less sensitive than extremes and likely redundant with phosphorus max in the same/nearby epoch.

Average phosphorus closest to the prediction horizon; sustained hyperphosphatemia can reflect evolving AKI. However, mean is typically dominated by peak phosphorus for capturing acute renal dysfunction.

Peak phosphorus in the immediate post-op period; an early spike can indicate impaired clearance or high cell breakdown. Timing is less reliable (transient post-bypass effects), so it’s a moderate sparse-model candidate.

Peak phosphorus during 13–24h, capturing early hyperphosphatemia from reduced renal clearance and evolving AKI. Max preserves acute insult information and is less noisy than 0–12h, making it a strong sparse feature.

Peak phosphorus in the closest available window; rising/peaking phosphorus is a near-direct marker of reduced GFR and AKI trajectory approaching Hour 60. Among phosphorus aggregations, this extreme and timing provide the most unique predictive value.
Lowest phosphorus early post-op, often driven by dilution, insulin shifts, or refeeding rather than renal dysfunction. Weak and potentially misleading for predicting creatinine rise, so low priority.
Minimum phosphorus in 13–24h; hypophosphatemia is generally not a direct precursor of AKI and is influenced by supplementation/shift physiology. Adds little unique value versus phosphorus max/mean.
Minimum phosphorus closest to Hour 60, still more reflective of shifts or replacement than impaired clearance. Unlikely to contribute in a sparse model compared with peak phosphorus.
Number of phosphorus checks in 0–12h reflects monitoring patterns and routine post-op labs. It is a weak proxy for illness complexity and not a direct renal signal.
Measurement frequency of phosphorus in 13–24h is primarily clinician behavior/acuity proxy. Typically redundant with actual phosphorus values and other severity markers.

More frequent phosphorus checks in 25–36h may indicate clinician concern for renal dysfunction/electrolyte derangements. Still a proxy and generally dominated by the phosphorus level itself, so only modest priority.

Average neutrophil count early post-op reflects inflammation/stress response and possible infection risk, which are indirect AKI contributors. Early timing and non-specificity make it a weak sparse-model feature.

Mean neutrophil count in 13–24h captures ongoing inflammatory burden, which can correlate with AKI risk but is indirect. Likely redundant with stronger hemodynamic/renal markers and thus low-to-moderate priority.

Peak neutrophil count in the immediate post-op window reflects inflammatory/stress response. It is an indirect AKI signal and early post-bypass inflammation is noisy, so low sparse-selection priority.

Peak neutrophil count at 13–24h may better reflect evolving infection/SIRS that can contribute to AKI. Still indirect and likely redundant with other severity markers, but more informative than 0–12h.
Lowest neutrophil count early post-op is usually not a direct renal hypoperfusion/AKI precursor. Likely noise or treatment-related and dominated by other physiologic signals.
Minimum neutrophil count at 13–24h is not a strong mechanistic predictor of creatinine rise compared with hemodynamics/renal markers. Low unique value for a sparse linear model.
Frequency of ANC measurement is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. It is not a physiologic renal signal and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
ANC measurement count reflects care intensity or concern rather than kidney function. Adds little unique predictive value beyond other acuity indicators.
Later ANC measurement frequency may proxy ongoing instability/infection workup closer to the outcome window. Still a behavior proxy, so only modest value.
Troponin measurement frequency early post-op is largely protocol-driven after cardiac surgery. It is a weak proxy for myocardial injury/low output and not a direct renal marker.

Troponin measurement frequency at 13–24h may reflect concern for perioperative MI/low cardiac output that can drive AKI. Still mostly clinician behavior and weaker than actual troponin values/hemodynamics.
Troponin measurement frequency at 25–36h could indicate ongoing concern for myocardial injury and potential renal hypoperfusion. However it remains a monitoring proxy with limited unique signal.

Average PaO2 in the first 12h reflects oxygenation/ventilation status but is not specific for renal injury. Early values are influenced by ventilator settings and perioperative management, limiting sparse-model value.
Mean PaO2 at 13–24h captures sustained oxygenation but remains an indirect illness-severity marker. Likely redundant with other respiratory/hemodynamic variables more proximal to AKI.

Mean PaO2 at 25–36h is closer to the 60h creatinine and may reflect ongoing hypoxemia/critical illness contributing to AKI risk. Still indirect, but timing makes it the best Po2 mean candidate in this batch.
Maximum PaO2 early post-op mostly reflects FiO2/ventilator strategy rather than physiology relevant to AKI. Highly confounded and low unique predictive value.
Peak PaO2 at 13–24h is driven by oxygen administration and does not capture hypoxic insult (min would be more informative). Redundant/low-value for sparse selection.
Peak PaO2 at 25–36h remains primarily a treatment artifact and not a renal-risk signal. Unlikely to add unique predictive information versus other features.

Lowest arterial oxygen tension in the immediate post-op window, reflecting hypoxemia severity. Indirect AKI risk signal and early post-bypass noise; likely dominated by hemodynamics/renal markers in a sparse model.

Minimum PaO2 in the 13–24h window, capturing hypoxemia after initial stabilization. Still an indirect illness-severity proxy for renal injury and usually redundant with stronger perfusion/vasopressor/acid-base signals.

Minimum PaO2 closest to the prediction horizon, reflecting ongoing hypoxemia that can worsen renal oxygen delivery. More temporally relevant than earlier epochs, but remains indirect and may not survive if stronger shock/renal features exist.
Count/frequency of PaO2 measurements in 0–12h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and ventilator/ABG workflow. This is a clinician-behavior proxy with little direct physiologic renal signal.
Frequency of PaO2 measurements in 13–24h, indicating acuity/monitoring rather than oxygenation itself. Low unique value for sparse selection compared with actual PaO2 extremes.

Frequency of PaO2 measurements in 25–36h, largely a proxy for ongoing instability or ventilator management. Typically redundant with measured physiologic derangements and weak for predicting creatinine ratio.
Average serum potassium in the first 12h, influenced by transfusions, bypass, insulin, and shifts. Early and smoothed, so it is less specific for evolving AKI than later or extreme potassium values.
Mean potassium in 13–24h, reflecting overall electrolyte control and renal handling. Some AKI signal but diluted by averaging and treatment effects; often redundant with potassium maxima/minima.

Mean potassium in 25–36h, closer to Hour 60 and may reflect sustained impaired renal excretion. More temporally informative than earlier means, but still likely dominated by potassium max (hyperkalemia episodes).

Peak potassium in 0–12h, capturing hyperkalemia episodes that can reflect early renal dysfunction or tissue injury. Useful extreme but early post-op shifts/hemolysis reduce specificity for Hour 60 creatinine ratio.

Peak potassium in 13–24h, a strong marker of impaired renal clearance and evolving AKI after initial stabilization. As an extreme, it preserves insult information and is a strong sparse-model candidate within this batch.

Peak potassium in 25–36h, the closest window and a near-direct signal of reduced renal excretion/ongoing AKI trajectory. High unique predictive value versus means/minima and likely to survive sparse selection.
Lowest potassium in 0–12h, often reflecting diuresis, insulin therapy, or dilution rather than renal injury. Early and treatment-driven, so weaker and less specific for predicting creatinine rise.
Minimum potassium in 13–24h, indicating hypokalemia risk and treatment effects. Generally less directly tied to AKI than hyperkalemia peaks and thus lower sparse priority.
Minimum potassium in 25–36h, still more reflective of supplementation/diuretics than renal failure physiology. Adds limited unique value compared with potassium_max in the same epoch.
Frequency of potassium measurements in 0–12h, primarily a monitoring/acuity proxy. Low direct renal signal and typically redundant with actual potassium values and other severity markers.

Count of potassium checks in 13–24h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinician-concern proxy rather than a renal biomarker. Likely redundant with actual potassium values and other severity signals, so low sparse-selection priority.

Frequency of potassium measurement in 25–36h may weakly reflect evolving AKI/arrhythmia risk prompting closer monitoring. Still a proxy variable and typically dominated by physiologic/lab values, so only modest value despite closer timing.

Average bilirubin in the immediate 0–12h window reflects early hepatic/cholestatic signal and peri-bypass effects more than kidney-specific injury. Mean is also less sensitive to acute insults and is early/noisy for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Peak bilirubin 0–12h can capture early hypoperfusion/hemolysis severity, which may correlate with later AKI risk. However it is indirect, early, and likely weaker than later-window organ dysfunction markers, so not a top sparse keeper.
Minimum bilirubin in 0–12h is not physiologically informative for renal risk and mostly reflects baseline/measurement noise. Strongly dominated by max/trajectory measures, so very low priority.
Number of bilirubin measurements in 0–12h is a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy. Early post-op ordering patterns are noisy and unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Bilirubin measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects concern for hepatic dysfunction/hemolysis rather than direct renal physiology. Typically redundant with actual bilirubin values and other severity indicators.

More frequent bilirubin checks in 25–36h may indicate ongoing organ dysfunction and higher acuity closer to the prediction horizon. Still a proxy and usually inferior to bilirubin level/extremes, so limited sparse-model value.

Mean RBC count 0–12h reflects anemia/hemodilution/transfusion context, which can relate indirectly to renal hypoxia and AKI risk. Early window and mean aggregation are relatively noisy/redundant, so weak-to-moderate priority.

Mean RBC count 13–24h captures ongoing anemia/hemodilution that may correlate with illness severity and renal risk. Still indirect and likely redundant with hematocrit/hemoglobin/transfusion variables, so not a sparse-model standout.

Mean RBC count 25–36h is closer to Hour 60 and may reflect sustained anemia affecting renal oxygen delivery and overall recovery trajectory. Among RBC means this is the most temporally relevant, but remains an indirect severity signal.
Maximum RBC count 0–12h is rarely a meaningful insult marker and may reflect hemoconcentration or lab variability. It is early and not clearly linked to AKI mechanisms, so low selection priority.

Maximum RBC count 13–24h is generally not a clinically actionable extreme for renal prediction and is susceptible to noise. Likely dominated by anemia (min) or transfusion-related features, so low value.
Maximum RBC count 25–36h is not a strong mechanistic predictor of AKI compared with hypotension/oliguria/creatinine trends. Even with better timing, it is a weak and redundant signal.

Minimum RBC count 0–12h captures nadir anemia/hemodilution/transfusion need, which can relate to renal hypoxia and perioperative complexity. Although early, the nadir is more informative than mean/max within this family.

Minimum RBC count 13–24h reflects persistent or worsening anemia after initial bypass-related shifts, a more stable signal linked to oxygen delivery and overall severity that can precede AKI. As an extreme and mid-window measure, it is the strongest RBC-derived candidate in this batch for sparse selection.

Lowest RBC count in the 25–36h window reflects anemia/hemodilution and possibly bleeding/transfusion burden, which can correlate with illness severity and renal hypoxia risk. Moderately useful but indirect for AKI and likely redundant with stronger perfusion/renal markers not shown here.

Indicates whether/how often RBC count was checked in the first 12h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and routine post-op labs. Weak direct renal signal and early window is noisy, so low sparse-selection priority.
Measurement frequency of RBC count in 13–24h is largely a clinician-behavior/acuity proxy rather than physiology. Unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse model.

RBC measurement frequency closer to the target may reflect ongoing bleeding/anemia evaluation or instability. Still primarily a monitoring proxy and dominated by actual RBC values (e.g., min), so only modest priority.

Whether D-dimer was measured early post-op reflects clinician concern for thrombosis/DIC rather than a direct kidney injury biomarker. Early timing and proxy nature make it a weak sparse-model candidate.
D-dimer measurement in 13–24h is mostly a workup/monitoring indicator (e.g., VTE concern) and not a direct renal physiology signal. Low likelihood of surviving sparse selection.

Later D-dimer measurement may indicate evolving complications (thrombosis, inflammation) that can co-occur with AKI. Still a clinician-behavior proxy without the actual value, so limited unique predictive value.

TEG ACT being measured early mainly signals bleeding/coagulopathy evaluation and transfusion management. It is indirect for renal outcomes and early post-op noise reduces predictive specificity for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
TEG ACT measurement in 13–24h remains a monitoring/workup proxy for coagulopathy rather than kidney function. Unlikely to add unique value in a sparse linear model.

Later TEG ACT measurement may reflect persistent bleeding/coagulopathy and higher overall complication burden, which can associate with AKI. Still proxy-only (no ACT value), so modest priority at best.
Mean immature granulocyte percentage reflects early inflammatory/stress response. It is a nonspecific illness-severity marker and early window is noisy, so low-to-moderate sparse utility.

Sustained elevation in immature granulocytes in 13–24h can indicate evolving infection/inflammation that increases AKI risk. Mean is less sensitive than max but this later window may carry some unique trajectory information.

Peak immature granulocyte percentage early post-op captures acute inflammatory surge. However, 0–12h values are often transient after surgery/CPB and are indirect for renal injury, limiting sparse-model priority.

Peak immature granulocyte percentage in 13–24h is a stronger signal of ongoing systemic inflammation/infection than the mean and may precede AKI. Within this batch it is the most informative version (max, later window), so high selection priority.
Minimum immature granulocyte percentage is not clinically meaningful for AKI risk and mainly reflects baseline/measurement noise. Dominated by max/mean aggregations and early timing.
Minimum immature granulocyte percentage in 13–24h provides little actionable inflammatory signal compared with max/mean. Redundant and unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.
Count of how often immature granulocyte % was checked in 0–12h; mainly reflects monitoring intensity rather than renal physiology. Weak, indirect proxy and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Measurement frequency of immature granulocyte % in 13–24h, a clinician-behavior/acuity proxy. Adds little unique value for AKI prediction compared with direct renal/hemodynamic signals.

Measurement frequency of immature granulocyte % in 25–36h; still a monitoring proxy rather than a biomarker. Even though closer to Hour 60, it is unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse model.
Whether TEG fibrinolysis was measured in 0–12h, reflecting bleeding/coagulopathy evaluation and practice patterns. Indirect for kidney injury and dominated by more proximal renal function measures.
Whether TEG fibrinolysis was measured in 13–24h; primarily indicates concern for coagulopathy/bleeding. Not a direct AKI precursor and unlikely to be selected when renal trajectory variables exist.
Whether TEG fibrinolysis was measured in 25–36h; still mostly a clinician-behavior marker. Limited mechanistic link to creatinine rise and low priority for sparse inclusion.
Average creatinine ratio (post-op/baseline) in 0–12h captures early renal function change. Useful but early post-op noise and redundancy with later windows/extremes reduce sparse-model priority.
Average creatinine ratio in 13–24h reflects early AKI trajectory beyond immediate bypass/anesthesia effects. Strong predictor but somewhat redundant with max and the closer 25–36h window.

Average creatinine ratio in 25–36h is the closest available summary of evolving renal dysfunction before the Hour 60 target. High direct relevance and likely to carry unique predictive signal in a sparse model.
Peak creatinine ratio in 0–12h captures early extreme renal insult. However, early transient changes are common post-op and later peaks/means are typically more predictive for Hour 60.
Peak creatinine ratio in 13–24h captures worsening AKI signal and is less confounded than 0–12h. Strong candidate, though partially redundant with 25–36h creatinine trajectory measures.

Peak creatinine ratio in 25–36h is a near-direct indicator of ongoing AKI close to the prediction horizon. Extremes often outperform means for capturing injury, making this top-tier for sparse selection.

Minimum creatinine ratio in 0–12h often reflects dilution/hemodilution or lab variability rather than injury. Less informative for future creatinine rise and dominated by max/mean trajectory features.
Minimum creatinine ratio in 13–24h may still reflect dilution or recovery rather than risk of later rise. Generally less predictive than max/mean and likely redundant in sparse selection.

Minimum creatinine ratio in 25–36h can indicate transient improvement or dilution, not necessarily protection from later AKI. Lower priority than contemporaneous mean/max which better encode injury trajectory.

Frequency of creatinine ratio measurement in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring/acuity proxy (sicker patients get more labs). Some weak predictive value via clinician concern, but dominated by actual creatinine values.

Count/frequency of creatinine ratio measurements in 13–24h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity or clinician concern rather than renal physiology. Weak, proxy-heavy signal and likely dominated by actual creatinine values/trajectory if available.

Frequency of creatinine ratio checks in 25–36h, closer to the Hour-60 target and may capture evolving AKI prompting repeat labs. Still a clinician-behavior proxy, but timing makes it a somewhat stronger sparse-model candidate than earlier measurement counts.

Whether hyperoncotic (25%) albumin was given early post-op, often for hypotension/third spacing or low oncotic pressure. Mostly a treatment/acuity proxy in a noisy immediate post-op window and not a direct renal marker.

25% albumin administration in 13–24h reflects ongoing hemodynamic/volume management and clinician response to instability. Indirect and confounded by indication; likely redundant with stronger perfusion/volume signals (MAP, vasopressors, urine output) if present.

25% albumin use in 25–36h may indicate persistent capillary leak/vasoplegia or attempts to augment intravascular volume near the prediction horizon. Still a therapy proxy, but later timing can add incremental information about unresolved shock/AKI risk.
Dose of 5% albumin in 0–12h is common routine post-bypass volume resuscitation. Highly nonspecific and early-window noise makes it unlikely to survive sparse selection.

5% albumin dose in 13–24h reflects continued volume resuscitation needs. Indirect and confounded by practice patterns; weaker than later-window signals and often redundant with net fluid balance/pressors.

5% albumin dose in 25–36h suggests ongoing intravascular volume support closer to Hour 60, potentially tracking persistent hypoperfusion risk. Still treatment-driven and not kidney-specific, but timing gives modest unique predictive value.
Analgesic dosing early post-op primarily reflects pain control/sedation strategy. Minimal mechanistic link to AKI and likely pure noise for creatinine ratio prediction.
Ongoing acetaminophen-codeine use is a comfort/transition-to-oral-care marker. Weak, indirect association with renal outcomes and unlikely to add value in a sparse model.

Later acetaminophen-codeine dosing mainly indicates recovery trajectory (awake, taking PO) rather than renal physiology. Any association is indirect and dominated by direct illness-severity/renal markers.
Acetaminophen dose in 0–12h reflects routine post-op analgesia/antipyresis. Not kidney-specific and unlikely to contribute unique predictive signal.
Acetaminophen dosing in 13–24h is common supportive care and weakly related to AKI risk. Sparse selection would favor direct renal/perfusion variables over this.
Acetaminophen use in 25–36h is a nonspecific marker of comfort care needs and recovery. Not a direct renal signal and likely redundant with more informative clinical trajectory features.
Allopurinol administration reflects chronic gout/hyperuricemia management rather than acute perioperative renal physiology. Little expected short-term predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Continuation of allopurinol in 13–24h is largely a home-med reconciliation signal. Weak, indirect, and unlikely to survive sparse feature selection for AKI prediction.

Allopurinol exposure in the 25–36h window is usually a chronic/home medication signal rather than an acute renal physiology marker. Likely weak, confounded by baseline gout/CKD and clinician behavior, and unlikely to add unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Aminocaproic acid use early post-op reflects antifibrinolytic/hemostasis practice and bleeding risk rather than kidney function directly. The 0–12h timing is noisy and this is mostly a perioperative management proxy, so low sparse-model priority.

Aminocaproic acid in 13–24h suggests ongoing bleeding/antifibrinolytic use, an indirect severity/management marker. Still not a direct renal hypoperfusion/AKI signal and likely redundant with stronger hemodynamic/renal features, so low priority.

Late (25–36h) aminocaproic acid may indicate persistent bleeding/re-exploration risk and associated renal stress, making it slightly more informative than earlier windows. However it remains an indirect clinician-behavior proxy and unlikely to survive sparse selection over direct perfusion/renal markers.

Amiodarone dose in 0–12h reflects treatment of atrial/ventricular arrhythmias and overall instability. It is an indirect severity marker and early post-op dosing is often protocolized, so limited unique value for predicting creatinine ratio.

Amiodarone in 13–24h suggests ongoing arrhythmia burden which can correlate with low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion. Still largely a treatment proxy and likely redundant with direct hemodynamics/vasopressor needs, so modest priority.

Amiodarone use/dose at 25–36h indicates persistent arrhythmia/instability closer to the Hour-60 outcome, which can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. Among the amiodarone epochs this is the most temporally relevant, though still indirect and treatment-confounded.

Amlodipine administration early post-op is typically continuation of chronic antihypertensive therapy and not a direct AKI signal. It is heavily confounded by home meds and perioperative holding practices, so very low sparse-model value.

Amlodipine in 13–24h mainly reflects resumption of home BP meds and clinician preference rather than renal physiology. Likely redundant with actual blood pressure/hemodynamic features and thus low priority.

Amlodipine at 25–36h still primarily indicates medication reconciliation/resumption rather than evolving kidney injury. Any association with creatinine ratio would be indirect and confounded, so not a sparse-model keeper.

Ampicillin use in 0–12h reflects antibiotic selection/prophylaxis or suspected infection rather than kidney function directly. Early post-op antibiotics are protocol-driven and provide little unique predictive signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Ampicillin in 13–24h may indicate suspected infection/sepsis, which can contribute to AKI risk. However it is a clinician-behavior proxy and likely dominated by direct inflammatory/hemodynamic markers, so only weak-to-modest priority.

Continued/new ampicillin at 25–36h can signal evolving infection closer to the outcome window, which may increase AKI risk. Still indirect and confounded by prescribing patterns, so not a top sparse feature.

Angiotensin II use early indicates refractory vasodilatory shock and high vasopressor requirement, a strong risk context for renal hypoperfusion. The 0–12h window is noisier, but this drug is relatively specific for severe shock, giving moderate unique value.

Angiotensin II dose in 13–24h reflects persistent vasoplegia/refractory shock beyond immediate post-op effects, closely tied to renal perfusion risk. This is a relatively specific severity marker and more likely to survive sparse selection than many medication proxies.

Angiotensin II requirement at 25–36h indicates ongoing refractory shock very near the prediction horizon, strongly implicating sustained renal hypoperfusion and evolving AKI. Within this batch it is the most temporally proximate and specific marker of severe circulatory failure, making it a top sparse-model candidate.

Early post-op apixaban exposure mainly reflects anticoagulation strategy/bleeding-thrombosis context rather than renal physiology. Weak and likely confounded by clinician choice; low unique value for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Apixaban dosing in this window can reflect recovery trajectory (able to take/receive anticoagulation) and baseline AF/VTE risk. Still an indirect clinician-behavior proxy with limited direct AKI signal, so low sparse-model priority.

Later apixaban use may indicate clinical stability and preserved hemostasis/renal function sufficient for anticoagulation decisions. It remains indirect and likely redundant with stronger severity/renal markers, so only modest value.

Immediate post-op aspirin reflects routine post-cardiac surgery antiplatelet practice and surgical team preference. Minimal direct linkage to evolving AKI and high redundancy with standard care patterns.
Aspirin in this period is still largely protocol-driven and more about bleeding risk tolerance than kidney perfusion/injury. Low unique predictive value for creatinine rise in a sparse model.

Continuation/resumption of aspirin by 25–36h can weakly proxy overall stability and absence of major bleeding/complications. Still indirect and dominated by direct renal/hemodynamic features, so low-moderate priority.

Early statin administration reflects chronic therapy continuation and routine secondary prevention rather than acute renal status. Little near-term mechanistic signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio and likely redundant with baseline comorbidity data.

Statin dosing here is mostly a care-process marker (med reconciliation/enteral tolerance). It provides minimal unique information about evolving AKI compared with hemodynamics, fluids, and renal labs.
Later statin continuation may weakly indicate clinical stability and ability to take enteral meds. Still an indirect proxy with low incremental predictive value for creatinine ratio.
Bisacodyl use is a bowel regimen marker and reflects sedation/immobility and routine ICU practice. It is not a renal biomarker and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Bisacodyl in this window remains a nonspecific care-process variable. Any association with AKI would be highly indirect and confounded, so very low priority.
Later bisacodyl administration still reflects bowel management rather than kidney injury or perfusion. Provides negligible unique predictive signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Bivalirudin use early post-op often indicates HIT concern/ECMO/complex anticoagulation needs, which can correlate with severe illness and AKI risk. However it is primarily a treatment-choice proxy and early timing is noisy, limiting sparse-model value.

Ongoing bivalirudin at 13–24h can mark persistent high-acuity support (e.g., mechanical circulatory support) and higher AKI risk. Still indirect, but more temporally informative than 0–12h and may add unique severity signal within this medication batch.

Bivalirudin use at 25–36h suggests continued complex anticoagulation needs and sustained critical illness close to the prediction horizon, which can track with evolving AKI. It remains a proxy variable but is the most informative epoch among the bivalirudin features.

Early bumetanide dose reflects diuretic requirement/diuretic responsiveness and attempts to manage volume overload or oliguria—signals closely tied to renal perfusion and evolving AKI. Despite early-window noise, diuretic need is a relatively direct renal/volume-status marker and a strong sparse-model candidate within this batch.

Loop diuretic exposure in 13–24h reflects attempts to manage volume overload/oliguria and can proxy evolving AKI or congestion. Moderately predictive but partly clinician-behavior driven and dominated by the closer 25–36h dose.

Loop diuretic dose in the closest window (25–36h) captures ongoing diuretic requirement/resistance near the outcome, often tracking worsening renal function or fluid overload. Strong sparse-model candidate among this batch due to temporal proximity and renal-relevant signal.

Early use of a phosphate binder mainly reflects pre-existing CKD/ESRD medication reconciliation rather than acute postoperative renal trajectory. In 0–12h it is especially noisy and largely redundant with baseline creatinine/comorbidity features typically available.

Calcium acetate use suggests underlying CKD/mineral bone disease and may correlate with higher risk of AKI/creatinine rise. Still mostly a chronic-disease proxy and medication-order dependent, so low priority for sparse selection.

Later phosphate binder administration continues to indicate chronic CKD/ESRD status rather than acute renal hypoperfusion. Slightly more informative by timing but still largely redundant and behavior-driven.

Calcium carbonate in the immediate postop period is commonly given for supplementation/antacid or chronic use and is not a direct AKI marker. High redundancy with other calcium products and weak mechanistic link to Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Ongoing calcium carbonate dosing is typically supplementation or chronic therapy and does not uniquely encode evolving kidney dysfunction. Likely redundant with other calcium administrations and low value for sparse modeling.
Later calcium carbonate dosing remains a nonspecific medication signal with minimal direct relationship to AKI trajectory. Even with closer timing, it is unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.

IV calcium chloride early postop often reflects hemodynamic instability, transfusion/citrate load, or electrolyte correction after bypass. It is an indirect severity/procedure proxy and early-window noise limits its unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine.

Calcium chloride use in 13–24h can indicate ongoing instability or significant electrolyte derangements, which may correlate with AKI risk. Still largely a treatment/acuity proxy and not kidney-specific, so low-moderate priority.

Calcium chloride requirement in 25–36h suggests persistent instability or major electrolyte issues closer to the outcome, which can track multi-organ dysfunction including AKI. More temporally relevant than earlier epochs, but remains indirect and clinician-driven.

IV calcium gluconate early postop is commonly used for electrolyte correction and may reflect bypass-related shifts or transfusion effects. It is a nonspecific severity/treatment marker and early timing reduces reliability for Hour-60 creatinine prediction.

Calcium gluconate in 13–24h may indicate ongoing electrolyte abnormalities or instability that correlate with AKI risk. However it is not kidney-specific and is partly redundant with calcium chloride and other acuity markers.

Later calcium gluconate dosing (25–36h) captures persistent electrolyte management closer to the outcome and may proxy ongoing critical illness associated with AKI. Still indirect and somewhat redundant with calcium chloride, so not top-tier.

Early carvedilol administration mainly reflects home beta-blocker continuation and clinician preference rather than renal physiology. In 0–12h it is especially confounded by perioperative holds and has little unique value for creatinine ratio.

Carvedilol dosing in 13–24h is largely a chronic therapy/heart-rate control signal and more about care patterns than AKI evolution. Weak, indirect association with renal outcomes and unlikely to be selected in a sparse model.

Beta-blocker exposure in the 25–36h window reflects hemodynamic management and underlying cardiac status rather than a direct renal insult. Likely redundant with direct perfusion/vasopressor and BP features (not shown here), so low sparse-selection priority.

Early cefepime use is mainly an infection/empiric-coverage and clinician-behavior signal, not a direct driver of AKI at this horizon. The 0–12h window is also noisy post-op and is dominated by later antibiotic exposure if informative.

Cefepime dosing at 13–24h may proxy evolving infection/severity and (if dose-adjusted) renal function, but it is indirect. Some potential signal, yet likely redundant with more direct renal/hemodynamic markers and with the later 25–36h cefepime feature.

Cefepime dosing closest to the outcome window can reflect ongoing infection severity and possible renal dose-adjustment as kidney function worsens. Among cefepime epochs this is the most temporally relevant, but it remains an indirect proxy rather than a renal biomarker.

Ceftriaxone exposure in the immediate post-op period is largely a prophylaxis/empiric-treatment marker and weakly related to subsequent creatinine rise. Early timing and lack of direct renal physiology make it a poor sparse-model candidate.

Ceftriaxone use at 13–24h may indicate suspected infection or care pathway differences, which can correlate with AKI risk indirectly. Still mostly a clinician-behavior/severity proxy and likely dominated by later (25–36h) exposure if any signal exists.

Later ceftriaxone use is the most temporally proximate antibiotic indicator here and may track infection burden or complications. However ceftriaxone is not strongly nephrotoxic and dosing is less tightly renal-adjusted than some agents, limiting unique predictive value.

Early clonidine reflects sedation/analgesia and BP control practices in the immediate post-op period. It is an indirect and noisy proxy for hemodynamics and is unlikely to add unique value beyond direct BP/vasopressor variables.

Clonidine at 13–24h may capture ongoing sympathetic control needs and hypotension risk, which can relate to renal hypoperfusion. Still indirect and typically redundant with measured MAP/vasopressor exposure, so modest priority.

Clonidine in the 25–36h window is closer to the creatinine outcome and could proxy sustained hemodynamic lability or sedation strategy. Nonetheless it remains a treatment-choice signal and is usually dominated by direct perfusion/hemodynamic features.

Early clopidogrel use mainly reflects antiplatelet strategy and bleeding/thrombosis considerations rather than kidney injury physiology. It has little direct mechanistic link to creatinine rise and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Clopidogrel at 13–24h is still primarily a procedural/antithrombotic management marker. Any association with AKI would be indirect (e.g., bleeding/transfusion pathways) and likely redundant with more direct variables.

Later clopidogrel exposure remains a weak proxy for renal outcomes and does not encode renal function directly. Even with better timing, it is unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse linear model.

Early colchicine use typically reflects pericarditis prophylaxis/treatment or inflammatory management, not renal perfusion or filtration. The 0–12h window is noisy and colchicine is not a strong AKI driver here, so very low priority.

Colchicine at 13–24h is an indirect marker of inflammatory/pericardial issues and clinician preference. It offers minimal unique information about evolving AKI compared with direct renal/hemodynamic features.

Colchicine closer to Hour 60 could weakly reflect ongoing inflammatory complications and (if held/reduced) clinician concern for renal dysfunction. Still largely a treatment-choice proxy with limited expected incremental value, so low-to-modest priority.

D10 in lactated Ringer’s given in the immediate post-op window, usually a nutrition/hypoglycemia-management choice rather than a renal physiology marker. Likely redundant with overall fluid strategy and early post-bypass noise, so low sparse-model priority.

D10 LR use in 13–24h reflects clinician preference for dextrose-containing balanced fluid rather than a direct AKI signal. Adds little unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio beyond broader hemodynamics/renal markers.

D10 LR use closer to the outcome could weakly proxy ongoing need for dextrose-containing maintenance fluids and illness complexity. Still largely a treatment-choice proxy and likely dominated by more direct renal perfusion/volume status features.

D10W administration early post-op is typically for hypoglycemia prevention/treatment or maintenance fluids. It is an indirect clinician-behavior signal and early timing is noisy for Hour-60 creatinine prediction.

Amount of D10W in 13–24h may reflect ongoing inability to tolerate enteral intake or glycemic management intensity. Some weak association with severity/ICU course, but not a direct renal insult marker and likely redundant with other acuity variables.

D10W use in 25–36h is temporally closer and may proxy prolonged critical illness or nutrition/insulin strategy. Still indirect and unlikely to survive sparse selection when direct renal/hemodynamic predictors are available.

Volume of D5 0.45% NS early post-op reflects maintenance fluid choice and contributes to fluid/chloride load. Could weakly relate to fluid balance and renal stress, but early window and treatment-choice nature limit unique predictive value.

Binary indicator of D5 0.45% NS use in 13–24h is a coarse proxy for maintenance fluid selection. As a binary and clinician-driven variable, it is likely redundant with total fluids/fluid balance and thus low priority.

Use of D5 0.45% NS in 25–36h may indicate ongoing maintenance fluids and could correlate with net fluid status approaching the outcome window. Still indirect and dominated by direct measures like urine output, creatinine trend, and hemodynamics.

D5 LR early post-op is mainly a maintenance fluid choice with dextrose and balanced electrolytes. It is not a direct kidney injury marker and is likely redundant with overall fluid administration patterns.
Binary D5 LR use in 13–24h reflects clinician preference rather than renal physiology. Low incremental value for sparse modeling compared with direct renal perfusion and kidney function features.

Amount of D5 LR in 25–36h captures ongoing IV fluid volume close to the prediction horizon and may relate to volume status/hemodilution and renal congestion risk. Among this batch it has relatively more usable signal (numeric + late window), though still indirect.

D5 NS early post-op is a common maintenance fluid and largely reflects routine care rather than evolving AKI. Early timing and strong confounding by clinician practice make it a weak sparse-model candidate.
Binary D5 NS use in 13–24h is a nonspecific treatment-choice indicator. It is likely redundant with total fluid balance and does not uniquely encode renal hypoperfusion or injury.

D5 NS use in 25–36h could weakly proxy ongoing IV fluid dependence and chloride exposure nearer to Hour 60. Still mostly clinician-behavior/maintenance-fluid signal and unlikely to beat direct renal/hemodynamic predictors in sparse selection.

D50W boluses early post-op typically indicate hypoglycemia treatment, which can correlate with severe illness or insulin management. It is an indirect proxy and early/noisy, but may carry a small unique severity signal compared with routine maintenance fluids.

D50W use suggests treatment of hypoglycemia or insulin-related glucose management rather than a direct renal signal. It is mostly a clinician-behavior/complexity proxy and likely redundant with stronger hemodynamic/renal predictors.

Later D50W use may reflect ongoing glycemic instability or nutrition/insulin titration closer to the outcome window. Still indirect for AKI and unlikely to survive sparse selection versus direct perfusion/renal markers.
Early D5W volume reflects postoperative fluid/nutrition practice and immediate bypass/anesthesia-related management. It is noisy in 0–12h and a weak, non-specific proxy for renal dysfunction.

D5W amount can correlate with overall fluid strategy and hypernatremia management, which may relate to kidney stress indirectly. However it is largely practice-pattern driven and redundant with more direct fluid balance and hemodynamic features.

Later D5W may capture ongoing free-water needs and fluid management closer to Hour 60. Still an indirect signal and typically dominated by net fluid balance, urine output, and renal labs in sparse models.

Dialysate infusion implies renal replacement therapy (e.g., CRRT) initiation, a near-direct indicator of severe AKI. Even early, this is a high-specificity marker likely to add unique predictive value for elevated creatinine ratio.

Ongoing/new dialysate infusion in 13–24h strongly indicates established AKI requiring RRT, tightly linked to subsequent creatinine trajectory. This is a top sparse-model feature due to specificity and strong effect size.

Dialysate infusion in the closest window (25–36h) is an especially strong, proximate marker of severe renal dysfunction and/or inability to clear solute. It should dominate this batch for predicting Hour 60 creatinine ratio.

Hydromorphone PCA dosing mainly reflects analgesia strategy and patient wakefulness rather than renal physiology. In 0–12h it is highly practice-dependent and unlikely to contribute unique predictive value.

PCA opioid dose is a weak proxy for sedation/respiratory status and nursing practice, not a direct AKI precursor. It is also redundant with total opioid exposure measures (e.g., hydromorphone dose) if present.

Later PCA dosing remains primarily a care-pattern variable and may even be inversely related to severity (sicker patients often cannot use PCA). It is unlikely to survive sparse selection for creatinine prediction.

Total hydromorphone dose reflects analgesia/sedation needs and clinician preference, with minimal direct linkage to renal hypoperfusion or injury. Early postoperative dosing is particularly noisy and redundant with PCA dosing.

Hydromorphone exposure may correlate with overall illness course but is an indirect, practice-driven signal. It is unlikely to add unique value compared with hemodynamics, vasopressors, urine output, and renal labs.

Later opioid dosing is still dominated by sedation/analgesia protocols and patient interaction rather than kidney function. Any association with creatinine is likely confounded and not sparse-model competitive.

Dobutamine use indicates low cardiac output support, a plausible mechanism for renal hypoperfusion after cardiac surgery. However 0–12h is confounded by transient post-bypass instability and may be redundant with direct MAP/CI/lactate signals.

Dobutamine requirement in 13–24h suggests persistent low-output state, which is a strong upstream driver of AKI and more stable than immediate post-op use. It can add unique predictive value if not already captured by detailed hemodynamics/vasopressor features.

Total dobutamine requirement in the 25–36h window reflects ongoing low cardiac output/cardiogenic shock physiology that can drive renal hypoperfusion and evolving AKI. Late inotrope dose is a strong, relatively unique severity/hemodynamic-support signal likely to survive sparse selection.

Docusate is a bowel regimen medication and does not directly reflect renal physiology. In the immediate 0–12h post-op period it is largely routine care/ordering behavior and adds negligible unique predictive value.
Docusate dosing mainly indicates bowel regimen use rather than kidney injury risk. Any association would be indirect (care patterns) and is unlikely to be selected in a sparse model.
Docusate use at 25–36h remains a non-renal, low-signal medication feature. It is dominated by true hemodynamic/renal markers and is unlikely to add unique predictive information.
