Early dopamine use reflects hemodynamic support needs, but 0–12h is noisy post-bypass and dopamine may be used variably (including low-dose practices), making it a weaker proxy for later AKI. Likely redundant with later vasoactive/inotrope requirements.

Dopamine requirement in 13–24h suggests persistent shock/low output that can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. Moderately useful, but often redundant with stronger late-window support signals (e.g., 25–36h inotropes/pressors) and practice-pattern variability.

Late (25–36h) dopamine dose indicates ongoing need for vasoactive/inotropic support close to the prediction horizon, aligning with sustained hemodynamic compromise that predicts creatinine rise. Strong sparse-model candidate, though still partly a clinician-behavior proxy and may overlap with other inotrope features.

Enoxaparin dosing primarily reflects VTE prophylaxis/anticoagulation decisions rather than renal injury physiology. Early post-op administration is highly protocol-driven and provides little unique signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Enoxaparin use can indirectly reflect renal function (dose holding/reduction in CKD/bleeding risk), but it is mostly a care-pattern proxy. Any predictive value is weak and likely dominated by direct renal/hemodynamic features.

At 25–36h, enoxaparin administration/withholding may weakly encode clinician assessment of bleeding risk or renal impairment, but remains indirect. Low priority for sparse selection compared with direct perfusion/AKI precursors.

Ephedrine use in 0–12h is typically transient anesthesia/early ICU bolus support and is highly practice-dependent. It is a noisy proxy for hypotension and unlikely to add unique predictive value for later creatinine rise.

Ephedrine use in 13–24h may indicate intermittent hypotension requiring boluses, which can relate to renal hypoperfusion. Still a weak, clinician-driven signal and generally dominated by continuous vasoactive/inotrope dose features.

Late ephedrine suggests episodic hypotension near the prediction horizon, but bolus use is variable and less quantitative than infusion doses. Provides limited incremental value beyond inotrope/pressor totals.

Esmolol use early post-op reflects rate control/arrhythmia management rather than renal perfusion directly. In 0–12h it is especially confounded by transient tachycardia and clinician preference, making it low-value for sparse selection.

Esmolol in 13–24h may mark atrial fibrillation/tachyarrhythmia management and indirectly illness severity, which can correlate with AKI risk. However it is an indirect proxy and likely redundant with hemodynamic support variables.

Late esmolol indicates ongoing rate control needs (e.g., AF with RVR) that can affect cardiac output, but the relationship to creatinine rise is indirect. Low-to-moderate priority and unlikely to beat direct hemodynamic support signals in a sparse model.

Etomidate exposure early post-op is mainly an anesthesia/sedation choice and can proxy hemodynamic instability rather than directly driving AKI. Early (0–12h) timing and indirectness make it a low-priority sparse feature.

Etomidate use in this window likely reflects ongoing sedation needs/clinical course rather than a direct renal signal. Some proxy value for severity, but still weak and likely dominated by direct hemodynamic/renal markers.

Late etomidate use may indicate prolonged critical illness or procedures, indirectly associated with AKI risk. Still a clinician-behavior proxy and unlikely to survive sparse selection versus direct perfusion/renal variables.

Ezetimibe is a chronic lipid-lowering medication and perioperative dosing is not a meaningful acute kidney injury driver. Likely noise/restart-of-home-meds signal with minimal unique predictive value.
Continuation/restart of ezetimibe reflects medication reconciliation rather than renal physiology. Very low priority for a sparse model predicting creatinine trajectory.
Late ezetimibe dosing remains a home-med continuation signal with little mechanistic link to AKI at 60h. Unlikely to add unique information beyond comorbidity/medication history features.

Famotidine is stress-ulcer prophylaxis; dosing may weakly proxy ICU protocol adherence or renal dose-adjustment. Early timing and strong confounding by routine care make it low-value for sparse selection.

Famotidine use/dose can reflect routine prophylaxis and occasional renal adjustment, but is mostly clinician-behavior/protocol driven. Limited unique predictive value compared with direct renal labs/urine output.

By 25–36h, famotidine dose reductions/holds may start to reflect evolving renal dysfunction (renal dosing) rather than pure routine care. Still indirect and confounded, but the later window gives it modest sparse-model utility.

Fentanyl PCA dose reflects analgesia needs and patient interaction; it is an indirect proxy for extubation status and illness severity. Early post-op analgesia is routine and noisy, so low sparse priority.

PCA dosing in this window may indicate recovery trajectory (awake/extubated) but remains an indirect severity/management marker. Likely redundant with stronger physiologic indicators of instability and renal hypoperfusion.

Later PCA use can capture recovery vs persistent critical illness (e.g., awake on PCA vs still ventilated), which correlates with AKI risk. Still indirect, but closer timing makes it a modest candidate if few trajectory features are available.

Total fentanyl (non-PCA) early post-op is largely driven by anesthesia/ventilation/sedation practices and immediate instability. It is an indirect proxy and likely redundant with direct hemodynamic/ventilator variables.
Ongoing fentanyl requirements may reflect prolonged ventilation or agitation, indirectly associated with AKI via severity. Still a management proxy with limited unique value for sparse selection.

Higher fentanyl needs at 25–36h can indicate persistent critical illness/ventilation and thus higher AKI risk by 60h. It remains indirect but is the most temporally relevant fentanyl aggregate in this batch.
Intranasal fluticasone is a chronic rhinitis medication with no meaningful acute renal relevance. Perioperative administration is largely incidental and unlikely to contribute predictive signal.
Intranasal steroid use reflects chronic rhinitis/allergy management rather than renal physiology. Very unlikely to add unique predictive value for AKI/creatinine trajectory in a sparse model.
Intranasal fluticasone dosing is a non-renal, low-acuity medication signal. Even though closer to Hour 60, it is essentially noise for creatinine_ratio and not a sparse-model keeper.

Folic acid administration is typically routine supplementation and not a marker of renal hypoperfusion or evolving AKI. Early post-op timing further reduces any plausible signal for Hour 60 creatinine_ratio.
Folic acid use mainly reflects baseline nutrition/med list continuation rather than kidney injury risk. It is a weak, indirect proxy and unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.
Later folic acid dosing still represents routine supplementation with minimal mechanistic link to AKI. Not expected to contribute unique predictive information for creatinine_ratio.

Loop diuretic dosing reflects volume management/oliguria response and can proxy early renal stress or congestion. However 0–12h is noisy post-bypass and dosing is clinician-behavior influenced, so it’s moderately useful but not top-tier.

Furosemide requirement in 13–24h often reflects evolving fluid overload, low urine output, or cardiorenal physiology relevant to subsequent creatinine rise. This window is more informative than 0–12h and may add unique signal beyond generic severity.

Furosemide dose at 25–36h is a near-term indicator of ongoing diuretic resistance/volume overload or oliguria management, closely tied to impending AKI and Hour 60 creatinine trajectory. Among this batch it is the strongest, most time-proximal renal-relevant feature and a high-priority sparse-model keeper.

Gabapentin dosing is primarily an analgesia/sedation adjunct and not a direct renal biomarker. Early post-op administration is routine and unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.
Gabapentin use mainly reflects pain regimen and clinician preference rather than renal perfusion or injury. Any association with creatinine is likely confounded and weak for sparse selection.
Later gabapentin dosing remains a non-specific medication signal with minimal mechanistic linkage to AKI prediction. Not competitive with diuretic/fluids for sparse inclusion.
Guaifenesin is an expectorant and largely unrelated to kidney function or hemodynamic drivers of AKI. Likely noise and redundant with overall medication burden proxies.
Guaifenesin administration reflects airway secretion management rather than renal physiology. Unlikely to survive sparse-model screening for creatinine_ratio.
Expectorant dosing at 25–36h is still not a meaningful predictor of renal dysfunction. Any signal would be indirect and dominated by more renal-relevant features.

Half-normal saline volume reflects early fluid administration and can relate to hemodilution/volume status, but 0–12h is heavily confounded by operative fluid shifts. As a clinician-behavior/volume proxy it is a weak candidate compared with later diuretic needs.

Half-normal saline given in 13–24h may capture ongoing fluid strategy and net volume exposure relevant to congestion or renal perfusion. Still indirect and potentially redundant with more direct measures (e.g., diuretic requirement), but more informative than 0–12h.

Represents hypotonic maintenance fluid volume given in the closest (25–36h) window. Mostly a clinician-behavior/volume-management proxy and weakly specific for AKI risk compared with direct renal perfusion/urine output/creatinine signals.
Early postoperative heparin exposure (0–12h), typically for anticoagulation protocols. Largely unrelated to renal physiology and early timing is noisy; unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Heparin dose in 13–24h reflects anticoagulation strategy rather than kidney injury trajectory. Provides little unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Heparin dose in 25–36h is still primarily protocol/indication driven (e.g., valve/AF/ECMO) and not a direct AKI precursor. Weak and indirect for sparse modeling.

Hydralazine use suggests treatment of postoperative hypertension in the immediate 0–12h period. Could weakly relate to hemodynamic management but is indirect and early-window noise limits predictive value.
Hydralazine in 13–24h indicates ongoing BP control needs. Still a treatment proxy and likely redundant with actual BP/hypotension metrics that better capture renal perfusion risk.

Hydralazine in 25–36h captures later hypertension management closer to the outcome window. May add modest unique signal about hemodynamic trajectory/afterload management, but remains indirect vs direct MAP/vasopressor features.

Opioid/acetaminophen analgesic dosing in 0–12h reflects pain control and extubation pathway rather than renal injury. Mostly clinician-behavior and early postoperative variability; low sparse-model priority.
Analgesic dosing in 13–24h is a proxy for pain/sedation strategy and recovery pace. Weakly connected to AKI and likely dominated by direct severity/hemodynamic variables.
Analgesic dosing in 25–36h mainly reflects comfort and care pathway (e.g., oral intake) rather than kidney function. Minimal unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Hydrocortisone use early (0–12h) often indicates vasoplegia/shock treatment or adrenal support. It is an indirect severity/vasopressor-resistance marker and early timing reduces reliability for Hour-60 AKI prediction.

Hydrocortisone in 13–24h suggests persistent vasoplegia/inflammatory shock physiology, which is associated with renal hypoperfusion risk. Still partly redundant with vasopressor dose/MAP but may add unique treatment-intensity signal.

Hydrocortisone in 25–36h indicates ongoing shock/vasoplegia close to the prediction horizon, a strong context for evolving AKI. Likely to retain unique value as a marker of persistent hemodynamic instability despite being a treatment proxy.
Hypertonic saline early (0–12h) is uncommon and usually indication-driven (e.g., hyponatremia/ICP), not a direct renal marker. Likely sparse and noisy with little generalizable predictive value.
Hypertonic saline in 13–24h remains a rare, indication-specific intervention. More reflective of specific electrolyte/neurologic management than AKI trajectory.

Hypertonic saline in 25–36h could reflect significant dysnatremia/volume strategy closer to outcome, which may correlate with illness complexity. Still rare and largely a clinician/indication proxy, so only low-moderate sparse priority.

Early postoperative NSAID exposure can contribute to renal hypoperfusion/AKI risk, but in cardiac surgery it is often minimal/avoided and 0–12h is noisy. Likely weak and partly a clinician-behavior proxy, so low sparse-selection priority.

NSAID dosing in the 13–24h window may better reflect ongoing analgesic strategy and potential prostaglandin-mediated renal risk than the immediate post-op period. Among ibuprofen epochs this is more informative, but still indirect and often low-variance.

Later (25–36h) NSAID exposure is closest to the outcome window and may capture sustained nephrotoxic/renal-perfusion stress when AKI is evolving. Within this batch it is the strongest ibuprofen signal and could add unique predictive value if NSAIDs are used.

Inhaled acetylcysteine is typically used for airway secretion management rather than renal protection. It is largely unrelated to kidney physiology and mainly reflects respiratory care intensity, so it is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Continued inhaled acetylcysteine use reflects pulmonary toileting needs and clinician practice, not a direct renal signal. Redundant across epochs and weakly connected to creatinine trajectory.

Late inhaled acetylcysteine dosing remains a respiratory-therapy proxy with minimal mechanistic link to AKI or creatinine rise. Even with better timing, it offers little unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.

Inhaled LABA use indicates obstructive lung disease management and is not a direct driver of postoperative renal dysfunction. Early dosing is especially protocol-driven and unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model.

Arformoterol dosing in this window is still primarily a COPD/asthma treatment marker and a care-pattern proxy. Any association with creatinine is indirect and likely dominated by hemodynamic/renal features not in this batch.
Later arformoterol use remains a respiratory comorbidity/therapy indicator with minimal causal connection to AKI. Timing is closer but the signal is still weak and redundant across epochs.

Inhaled corticosteroid dosing reflects chronic airway disease management and ICU respiratory regimen rather than renal physiology. Early post-op administration is routine and not informative for creatinine_ratio.

Budesonide use is a pulmonary therapy marker with little direct relationship to evolving kidney injury. Likely redundant with other respiratory-medication indicators and low priority for sparse selection.

Late inhaled budesonide remains an indirect proxy for respiratory comorbidity/ventilator course rather than renal insult. Even with better temporal proximity, it is unlikely to contribute unique predictive signal for creatinine_ratio.
Short-acting bronchodilator dosing reflects bronchospasm/airway management and is not a kidney-specific marker. Early dosing is common and protocolized, making it a weak, indirect predictor.

Levalbuterol use in this window is mainly a respiratory status/therapy proxy and may correlate with illness severity only indirectly. It is unlikely to add unique value compared with true renal/hemodynamic predictors.

Later levalbuterol dosing still primarily captures pulmonary care needs and clinician behavior rather than renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxicity. Despite closer timing, it remains low-yield for sparse modeling of creatinine_ratio.
Inhaled saline is used for secretion clearance/humidification and does not directly inform kidney function. It is largely a respiratory-care proxy and early post-op use is noisy and non-specific.

Inhaled saline is a respiratory secretion/airway clearance therapy. It has no direct renal physiology signal and mainly reflects ventilator/airway management, so it is very low priority for sparse AKI prediction.
Inhaled saline dose in the closest window is still primarily a pulmonary care proxy. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio compared with hemodynamics/renal markers.
Inhaled levalbuterol reflects bronchospasm/airway reactivity treatment early post-op. It is an indirect severity/respiratory-management marker and early timing is noisy for Hour-60 renal outcomes.
Levalbuterol use in 13–24h indicates ongoing respiratory therapy needs. This is largely unrelated to kidney injury mechanisms and is dominated by more direct perfusion/renal features.
Levalbuterol in 25–36h may proxy persistent critical illness (e.g., prolonged ventilation) closer to the target time. Still indirect and clinician-behavior driven, so only a weak candidate.
Basal insulin dosing reflects diabetes status and glycemic management rather than acute renal physiology. Early post-op dosing is often protocolized and noisy, limiting sparse-model value.

Glargine dose in 13–24h continues to reflect baseline diabetes and insulin strategy. It may weakly correlate with comorbidity burden but is indirect and likely redundant with other diabetes indicators.
Later glargine dosing is closer to Hour 60 but still mainly represents chronic diabetes/feeding status and protocol choices. It provides limited unique information for AKI beyond more direct signals.
Detemir (basal insulin) dose is another representation of diabetes management. It is largely redundant with glargine as a basal-insulin signal and not directly tied to evolving AKI.
Detemir dosing in 13–24h reflects ongoing basal insulin needs. It is an indirect comorbidity/protocol marker and redundant with other insulin features, so low sparse-selection priority.
Detemir in 25–36h is temporally closer but still a weak proxy for illness/diabetes rather than renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxic exposure. Redundant with other basal insulin variables.

Rapid-acting insulin dose reflects hyperglycemia and nutritional status in the immediate post-op period. It is indirect for AKI and early values are heavily influenced by stress response and protocols.

Lispro dose in 13–24h can capture sustained stress hyperglycemia/insulin resistance, which correlates with complications and AKI risk. Among insulin features it may add more dynamic information, but remains indirect.

Lispro dose in 25–36h is the closest window and may reflect ongoing stress hyperglycemia, feeding, and severity that track with AKI trajectory. Still a proxy and potentially confounded by clinician targets, so moderate priority.

Regular insulin infusion dose indicates need for continuous insulin (often severe stress hyperglycemia) and ICU intensity. It can correlate with systemic illness that predisposes to AKI, but early post-op dosing is protocol-driven and noisy.

Ongoing insulin infusion requirement in 13–24h suggests persistent stress hyperglycemia/critical illness and may track with inflammatory/vasopressor trajectories linked to AKI. Within this batch it is one of the more informative, less redundant dynamic signals for sparse selection.

Total regular insulin infusion over 25–36h reflects stress hyperglycemia/insulin resistance and overall illness severity. It can correlate with AKI risk but is an indirect proxy and likely redundant with stronger hemodynamic/renal-perfusion signals, so moderate sparse-model priority.

Exposure to iodinated contrast (iopamidol) in the first 12h can contribute to nephrotoxicity. However this very early window is noisy post-op and contrast use is often procedure-driven; keep low unless contrast is common/variable in this cohort.

Contrast exposure in 13–24h is temporally closer to evolving AKI and may add unique nephrotoxic signal beyond baseline risk. Still binary and relatively infrequent/procedure-driven, so moderate priority.

Contrast exposure in 25–36h is the closest window to the Hour-60 creatinine and can directly precipitate or worsen AKI. Among the contrast indicators, this timing is most informative and least redundant, making it a strong sparse-model candidate.

Ipratropium dosing reflects bronchodilator use for airway disease/bronchospasm rather than renal physiology. It is largely a treatment/behavior proxy and early post-op use is unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
Bronchodilator dosing in 13–24h mainly captures pulmonary comorbidity or ventilatory course. It is indirect and likely redundant with more direct severity markers, so very low priority.
Ipratropium use in 25–36h remains a pulmonary-treatment proxy with minimal mechanistic link to AKI. Even with closer timing, it is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Combined ipratropium/albuterol dosing early post-op reflects bronchospasm/COPD management and clinician practice. It provides little direct renal signal and is dominated by more proximal kidney/perfusion features.
Bronchodilator combination dosing in 13–24h is an indirect marker of respiratory issues and care intensity. It is unlikely to add unique information for Hour-60 creatinine beyond general severity.
Bronchodilator combination dosing in 25–36h remains a non-renal treatment proxy. Despite temporal proximity, it is weakly related to AKI and not a sparse-model keeper.

Iron administration early post-op reflects anemia management/transfusion strategy rather than kidney injury. It is largely clinician-behavior driven and unlikely to contribute unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
Iron dosing in 13–24h is a proxy for anemia/bleeding management and practice patterns. Any association with AKI would be indirect and redundant with stronger hemodynamic/bleeding indicators.
Iron dosing in 25–36h still primarily captures anemia treatment decisions rather than renal physiology. Low expected incremental value for sparse selection.

Isosorbide mononitrate use indicates chronic CAD/antianginal therapy and potential vasodilation affecting blood pressure. Early post-op continuation/holding is practice-driven and indirect for AKI, so low priority.
Nitrate use in 13–24h may reflect hemodynamic tolerance and underlying cardiac disease. Still a medication proxy with unclear directionality and likely redundancy with direct BP/vasopressor features.

Nitrate use in 25–36h could weakly signal ongoing cardiac ischemia management and vasodilatory exposure near the prediction horizon. However it remains indirect and clinician-driven, so only a low sparse-model candidate.

Potassium phosphate repletion early post-op, often triggered by hypophosphatemia and/or electrolyte management. Weak, clinician-driven proxy for illness/renal handling and likely noisy in 0–12h; may add limited unique value.

KPhos use in 13–24h reflects ongoing electrolyte derangements and care intensity. Still an indirect treatment signal and likely redundant with direct labs (phosphate/K/Cr) if available, so low sparse-model priority.

KPhos repletion closest to the outcome window may reflect persistent renal tubular dysfunction, refeeding, or ongoing critical illness. As a late treatment proxy it can add some incremental signal, but remains indirect and confounded by practice patterns.

Ketamine dose in the immediate post-op period mainly reflects sedation/analgesia strategy and hemodynamic tolerance. It is not a direct renal signal and is likely dominated by stronger hemodynamic/vasopressor features.
Ketamine use in 13–24h is largely a clinician-behavior marker (sedation choice, delirium/pain) rather than kidney physiology. Unlikely to survive sparse selection for predicting creatinine ratio.
Ketamine dose at 25–36h still primarily captures sedation practice and ongoing ventilation needs. Any association with AKI would be indirect and confounded, so minimal unique predictive value.

Early LR volume reflects resuscitation and fluid exposure, which can affect renal perfusion and creatinine dilution. However 0–12h is noisy post-bypass and fluid dose is a treatment proxy often redundant with net fluid balance/pressors.

LR volume in 13–24h may better reflect ongoing hypoperfusion/resuscitation needs and fluid loading that relates to AKI risk. Moderate sparse-model value, though still confounded and partly redundant with balance and hemodynamics.

Late LR administration (25–36h) is closer to Hour-60 creatinine and may indicate persistent shock/low output or attempts to support renal perfusion. Useful but indirect and likely overlaps with vasopressor dose, MAP, and fluid balance features.

Lactulose use early post-op typically reflects bowel regimen or hepatic encephalopathy management rather than renal physiology. Mostly clinician-behavior and unlikely to add unique predictive signal for AKI.
Lactulose in 13–24h is generally a constipation/bowel protocol marker. Minimal mechanistic link to creatinine rise and low priority for sparse selection.
Lactulose at 25–36h remains a nonspecific care-process indicator. Any association with creatinine ratio would be indirect (illness complexity), so very weak candidate.
Levothyroxine dosing reflects chronic hypothyroidism replacement and medication reconciliation. Not a near-term driver of postoperative AKI and unlikely to contribute unique predictive value.
Continuation of levothyroxine in 13–24h is a stable chronic-med marker. Little relevance to acute renal trajectory and dominated by direct renal/hemodynamic variables.
Levothyroxine at 25–36h remains a chronic therapy indicator with minimal acute physiologic linkage to creatinine changes. Very low sparse-model priority.
Topical lidocaine patch use is an analgesia choice and care-process marker. No direct renal signal and likely unrelated to Hour-60 creatinine ratio beyond weak confounding.
Topical lidocaine patch use reflects analgesia choice rather than renal physiology. Likely weak, behavior-driven signal with minimal unique value for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Topical lidocaine patch dosing in the closest window is still primarily a pain-management proxy. Unlikely to add independent predictive information about evolving AKI in a sparse model.

Systemic lidocaine early post-op is typically for analgesia/antiarrhythmic use and is not a direct kidney injury marker. Early window plus high confounding by practice patterns makes it a low-priority sparse feature.

Systemic lidocaine dose may correlate with arrhythmia/pain management but is an indirect, clinician-driven signal. Likely dominated by hemodynamics/vasopressors and other severity markers not in this batch.

Later systemic lidocaine could weakly proxy ongoing arrhythmia management or instability, which can relate to renal hypoperfusion. Still indirect and likely redundant with stronger physiologic predictors, so low priority.

ACE-inhibitor exposure can reduce glomerular filtration pressure and is often held in AKI/hypotension, making it a treatment/selection proxy. In the immediate 0–12h window it is noisy and practice-dependent, so only weakly useful.

ACE-inhibitor continuation/restart by 13–24h may reflect hemodynamic stability and clinician assessment of renal risk, and can also contribute to creatinine rise. Moderately informative but still confounded by indication and management decisions.

ACE-inhibitor dosing in the 25–36h window is closer to the Hour-60 outcome and can both signal stability (being restarted) and physiologically affect creatinine via efferent arteriolar dilation. Among this batch, it offers relatively unique renal-relevant information and is a strong sparse-model candidate.

ARB exposure has similar renal hemodynamic effects as ACE inhibitors but early post-op administration is uncommon and highly practice-driven. The 0–12h timing is noisy, limiting sparse-selection value.

ARB use by 13–24h may indicate recovery/stability and can contribute to creatinine increases, giving some predictive signal. However it is largely a clinician-behavior proxy and overlaps with ACE-inhibitor information.

ARB dosing in the closest window can meaningfully influence creatinine and also reflects a decision to resume RAAS blockade, which is tied to renal/hemodynamic status. Strong within-batch candidate, though partially redundant with lisinopril if both are present.

Magnesium sulfate replacement early post-op is routine and mainly reflects electrolyte management rather than kidney injury. High practice-pattern noise and weak direct linkage to Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Ongoing magnesium sulfate dosing is typically protocolized replacement and not a direct renal biomarker. Likely redundant with serum magnesium levels (not shown) and low unique predictive value.

Later magnesium sulfate needs could weakly reflect renal handling (less replacement if AKI) or arrhythmia burden, but it remains an indirect treatment proxy. Slightly higher due to temporal proximity, yet still low priority for sparsity.

Non-sulfate magnesium dosing early post-op is generally routine supplementation and not specific for renal dysfunction. Early timing and redundancy with other magnesium formulations make it a poor sparse feature.
Magnesium supplementation in 13–24h is still largely protocol-driven and weakly connected to AKI compared with direct renal/hemodynamic variables. Low unique predictive value within this batch.

Represents magnesium repletion in the 25–36h window, usually driven by electrolyte monitoring/arrhythmia prophylaxis rather than renal physiology. May weakly proxy evolving renal dysfunction (repletion patterns/avoidance), but is indirect and likely dominated by direct renal/hemodynamic features.
Melatonin use early post-op is mainly a sleep/delirium management choice. It is a clinician-behavior proxy with little direct linkage to AKI risk and is far from the 60h target.
Melatonin dosing reflects sedation/sleep protocol rather than kidney injury biology. Even though closer in time than 0–12h, it remains a weak, non-specific proxy unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Melatonin in the closest window still primarily indicates sleep/delirium management and ICU practice style. Minimal unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio compared with direct renal perfusion/renal function markers.

Meropenem early post-op suggests suspected/treated infection or high acuity, which can correlate with later AKI. However it is largely an indication/behavior proxy and early timing is noisy; dose also depends on renal function but is not a clean signal.

Meropenem use at 13–24h more plausibly reflects evolving sepsis/complications that can drive AKI by 60h. Still indirect and confounded by prescribing practices, but may add some unique severity/infection signal in a sparse model.

Meropenem in the 25–36h window is closer to the outcome and may capture ongoing infection/organ dysfunction trajectory relevant to later creatinine rise. Remains an indirect proxy and potentially confounded by renal-dose adjustment, so not top-tier.

Methocarbamol is used for muscle spasm/pain control and does not directly inform renal perfusion or injury. Early post-op dosing is largely protocol-driven and unlikely to add unique predictive value.
Continued methocarbamol use reflects analgesia strategy rather than kidney dysfunction. Any association with AKI would be weak and mediated by overall care patterns, so low sparse-selection priority.
